annotations.text: Five years of aromatase-inhibitor therapy, either as adjuvant therapy or as sequential therapy after tamoxifen, may not be sufficient to reduce the risk of late recurrence but remains the standard of care at this time.
Resistance to endocrine therapies is associated with a shift in tumorigenic signaling through alternative pathways.
Late recurrence of HR-positive breast cancer is an important clinical problem.

However, three trials currently under way may help to determine the appropriate duration of adjuvant therapy with an aromatase inhibitor: the National Cancer Institute Canada (NCIC) MA17R trial (ClinicalTrials.gov number, ), the National Surgical Adjuvant Breast and Bowel Project (NSABP) B42 trial, and the Secondary Adjuvant Long-term Stu
The PI3K–AKT–mTOR pathway has been associated with resistance to endocrine therapies in patients with breast cancer.
We really do not know, but it is an interesting question.
The Nurses’ Health Study looked at aspirin use in women who had previously had breast cancer, and it appeared that women who used aspirin actually had a reduced risk of breast-cancer recurrence.

The use of exemestane or fulvestrant alone in the treatment of this patient is supported by the Evaluation of Faslodex versus Exemestane Clinical Trial (EFECT), which showed equivalent but modest efficacy of the drugs, with a median time to disease progression of 3.7 months.

However, the Breast Cancer Trials of Oral Everolimus-2 (BOLERO-2) showed an increase in progression-free survival from 4.1 months to 11 months among patients receiving exemestane plus the mTOR inhibitor everolimus (as compared with patients receiving exemestane alone) but also showed increased toxic effects among these patients, with higher rates of stomatitis, rash, fatigue, diarrhea, decreased appetite, nausea, and weight loss.
The role of the PR is an area of active research.
